prose: Chișinău for two nights at Berd’s Hotel. Karaoke at Cooper, a walking tour along Stephen the Great Boulevard (Central Park, the Triumphal Arch, Kishinev Square), dinner at Pegas, cocktails at Marlene. The real point: Cricova and Purcari wineries, the latter two hours outside town, both worth it.
activities: Stephen the Great Boulevard walking tour

prose: Drove in from Lake Ohrid, crossed at Dogana Qafe Thane in thirty minutes. Tirana was in the middle of a Colour Day Festival when we arrived — murals, music, open squares. Lunch at ODA Garden, the two sobering museums (House of Leaves on surveillance, Bunk'Art 2), then back to the festival for dinner and drinks.

Cable car to Dajti on day two — paintball, mini golf, horses, a view. Walked down, stopped at Komiteti Kafe Museum for the traditional Albanian rakía, then an Italian dinner near the Fortress of Justinian. Left the next morning for Montenegro.
dining: Italian dinner near Fortress of Justinian
activities: Cable car to Dajti National Park

prose: Five days on the canals. Waldorf Astoria for the first half, De L'Europe for the second. Caviar pasta at The Duchess, a pizza night at Nnea, a proper dinner at Daalder, tulips at Keukenhof in April. The bar scene was stronger than we expected: Flying Dutchman, Pulitzers, Rosalia’s Menagerie, Door 74. Heineken Factory tour, Rijksmuseum, Anne Frank House, windmills day trip.

Puerto Williams, across the Drake Passage, and into the seventh continent. Mandatory lifeboat drill, kayak safety briefing, biosecurity inspection of all gear on day two. The Drake was mercifully tame — whale watching from the panorama lounge, naps, gym sessions.

The peninsula itself: Danger Islands for penguins and leopard seals, D'Urville Monument at 5am for the bay ringed in snow-covered mountains, Active Sound threading between glaciers, Orne Harbor for humpbacks and a penguin-colony hike, Damoy Point by late afternoon. Kayaking in snow and rain. Jacuzzi after, because it's on the ship.
dining: All dining onboard the expedition ship
activities: Humpback whale viewing from the bow

prose: Buenos Aires, Bariloche, Mendoza. BA is a city for walking — Recoleta, Palermo, Puerto Madero — and for steak. Polo clinic at The Jockey Club in San Isidro was the highlight; we learned polo basics from the captains, Patricio and Pablo, and spent an afternoon clumsily swinging mallets.
Bariloche to Las Balsas at Villa La Angostura for the lake district — Relais & Châteaux, chocolate boxes, fjord-like scenery. Mendoza for the wine, which is the right way to end most trips.
hotels: Park Hyatt Palacio Duhau, Buenos Aires
activities: Polo clinic at The Jockey Club, San Isidro

prose: New Year's Eve from the Park Hyatt rooftop — directly across from the Sydney Opera House — is one of the best fireworks shows you can buy a bed for. Bondi Icebergs for the morning-after swim, Bronte walk for the post-swim walk, Blue Mountains for the day out, Royal Botanic Gardens for the Jan 1 jog.

Melbourne for the food: David's hot pot, Tipo 00, Byrdi for cocktails. Then Cairns and out to Lizard Island — one airstrip, thirty villas, and the Great Barrier Reef off the beach. Swim off Blue Lagoon. Don't try to see everything in Australia. It's a continent.
activities: New Year's Eve fireworks from Park Hyatt rooftop
Blue Mountains — Three Sisters at Echo Point

prose: Two trips. Salzburg and Fuschl for the first — Schloss Mönchstein with its pool looking over the city, dinner at St. Peter Stiftskulinarium (claimed oldest restaurant in the world). Vienna the second time, Rosewood for base. Mulled wine at the Wiener Christkindlmarkt, Schloss Schönbrunn, an opera at the Vienna State Opera.
dining: St. Peter Stiftskulinarium (arguably the oldest restaurant)

prose: Baku at the Four Seasons. Old Town walk, Palace of the Shirvanshahs, Deniz Mall for the slide, a stroll through Mini Venice, the promenade. The Carpet Museum is shaped like a rolled-up carpet — worth the photo.
drinking: Bentley Bar at the Four Seasons

prose: One overnight. Ferry from the Four Seasons to the mall across the harbor for shopping, snacks at the Wolfgang Puck Lounge, dinner at Wolfgang Puck’s Asian spot in the hotel. Separate male/female gyms on some floors. Next morning, Pakistani currency at the bank before the flight to Lahore.
dining: Wolfgang Puck Asian at the Four Seasons
activities: Ferry to the mall across the harbor

prose: Ubud was slower than expected, in the best way. Morning yoga at COMO Shambhala, a hike down to the river at the bottom of the property, water purification at Tirta Empul where you plunge your head through a series of fountains and come up feeling like the day before was yesterday's problem.

We drove out to the rice terraces at Tegallalang, walked through the Sacred Monkey Forest (hold on to your sunglasses), and spent an afternoon at the market buying things we would fail to bring home. Bali doesn't feel crowded until it does. Ubud, then Seminyak, then the quieter parts — in that order.
activities: Guided walk through local rice fields

prose: Dhaka, two nights at the InterContinental. A proper city tour with a local guide: Dhakeshwari Hindu Temple, Lalbagh Fort, Dhaka New Market, the Liberation War Museum. Dinner at The Amber Room in the hotel.
dining: The Amber Room at the InterContinental

prose: Drove from Luxembourg to Brussels, stayed at Hotel Amigo in the Pentagone. La Grand Place is the centerpiece. Syrian dinner at Wolf (killer food hall), Italian dinner at À l'Angolo, drinks at the Green Man and then at a dive called De Monk. Then a flight to New York for new passport books.

Blue Hole either by dive or helicopter — both work, the helicopter gets the postcard shot. ATM Cave for the Mayan artifacts and skeletons preserved in the cavern. One of the most concentrated adventure-travel destinations in Central America.
activities: ATM Cave — Mayan artifacts and skeletons

prose: Budapest to Bucharest by rail — two nights and three days on the reimagined Orient Express. Pre-board from the Four Seasons Gresham Palace; boat tour on the Danube to meet the fellow passengers; Parliament tour the next morning; then the train.

The cabins are custom — Veuve Clicquot bottled for the train, green velour dining car, piano car, separate men's and women's dinner one night. Offboard excursions at Sinaia for Peleș Castle. Bucharest by the second afternoon. The train is the trip.
hotels: Belmond Venice-Simplon Orient Express (on the rails)

prose: We flew in on Drukair from Kathmandu into Paro — the world's second most dangerous airport — and were met by Amankora. Bhutan is the only country that measures Gross National Happiness. After a week there, you understand why.

Thimphu first: the Tashichho Dzong during Descending Day of Lord Buddha, an archery match where the men trash-talk their losses like Americans at a Packers game, and dinners of chili‑and‑cheese that don't quite leave your head. Then the two‑hour drive to Paro, the hike up to Tiger's Nest at dawn, and a farewell blessing from a monk by candlelight. The country is small, slow, and one of the few places left that feels intact.
dining: Traditional Bhutanese dinner at Amankora — chili, cheese, rice
Lunch at the hike-side villa near Tiger's Nest
drinking: Evening cocktails by the fire at Amankora
activities: Wangditse Lhakhang hike (2.5 miles, private blessing from the monk)
Ceremonial monk blessing at hotel departure

prose: Uyuni by chartered flight from Arequipa — fifteen handlers plus drug-sniffing dogs before boarding. Steak lunch at altitude. An hour's walk from the hotel straight into the salt flat, a local dog joining us for the whole thing.

Next day, train graveyard first, then Incahuasi — the cactus-covered island in the middle of the flat. Salt mirrors after the rain make the best photos in South America. The hotel at base was surprisingly full — there was apparently a Miss Bolivia commotion happening at lunch.
hotels: Palacio de Sal (built from salt bricks)
dining: Hotel dining at Palacio de Sal
drinking: Sunset on the salt flat with a flask

prose: Five hours from Dubrovnik, across Metkovic in about two minutes. Stopped at Kravica waterfalls for a swim; Mostar for the famous stone bridge and the kids who dive off it into the green river below. Riverside café lunch at exactly the right table.

Sarajevo is layered: Ottoman old town, Austro-Hungarian arcades, brutalist socialist blocks, the Olympic bobsled course crumbling at the top of Trebević, Latin Bridge where the Archduke was shot. Museum of Crimes Against Humanity & Genocide for the Bosnian War. Piccolo Mondo for dinner. A city that earns repeat visits.
activities: Mostar old town & the stone bridge
Museum of Crimes Against Humanity & Genocide

prose: Three camps: Mombo (Moremi), Vumbura (Okavango Delta), Duma Tau (Linyanti). Mombo delivered one of the best safari moments we've had — sixteen wild dogs streaming through the bush before lunch, a male lion resting over a downed water buffalo by afternoon.

Vumbura had water: speedboats through reed channels, mokoro (canoe) at sunset, elephants wading across the channel at arm's reach. Elephants surrounded our room one night — crack on the deck at 2am, one of them close enough to back away from in the morning. Duma Tau at the end was the most modern facility and the quietest. We'd go back.
activities: Game drive at Mombo — 16 wild dogs + lion with kill
Morning drive with lioness + fresh kill at Vumbura

prose: Three stops: São Paulo, Iguazu Falls, Rio. São Paulo is a food city — sushi at Nakka, Michelin one-star Evvai in Pinheiros, and then Tan Tan Noodle Bar, which we actually came for. Walked Ibirapuera Park.

Iguazu Falls from the Brazilian side is the better side (you look at the walls of water; the Argentinian side you look from them). Hotel inside the park so we could walk out after the tour buses left. Then Rio: Christ the Redeemer, Sugarloaf, Ipanema and Copacabana. Brazilians don't arrive on Sunday flights; we were basically alone at the airport.
activities: Iguazu Falls from the Brazilian side

prose: Borneo during Ramadan — sundown fast ends timing everything we did. The Empire Hotel (self-described as seven stars, opened 2000) has 522 rooms, a movie theater (where we watched a film and napped), and a buffet that comes alive at 6:45pm.

Tour with a local university student: Jame’asr Hassanil Bolkiah Mosque (exterior only, a Ramadan thing), Kampong Bakut Berumput floating village on stilts, a drive past the Sultan’s palace Istana Nurul Iman. Brunei is a peculiar country to visit. We liked it.

Sofia for three days. Intercontinental as base. Walked to the Cathedral of Saint Alexander Nevsky, ate pizza at Franco's, burgers at Skapto, wandered through South Park. Ancient Serdica archaeological complex doesn't wow for the ruins — it wows because you can feel the city's layers.

The Red Flat is worth the ticket: a communist-era apartment preserved as a museum in the middle of town. Dinner on Sofia Center, which looks and sounds like the Third Street Promenade. Quick drive onward to Skopje with a thirty-minute border.

We woke up at 4:30am to make Angkor Wat for sunrise, which is exactly the right move. Two hours later we were inside the best temple in the world with a hundred of our new best friends. Then Ta Prohm — the one Tomb Raider used, trees growing through the stone — and Angkor Thom, one of the largest religious structures on earth.

Siem Reap also: sunset boat on Tonlé Sap through floating villages, tuk-tuk ride to Pub Street which is exactly what it sounds like, silk workshop tour, and a massage to close every day. Our driver met us in a 1965 Mercedes. Cambodia is gentler than you expect.
activities: Tonlé Sap sunset boat & floating villages

prose: A multi-island ranking in rough order of favorite: St. Barths at the top (Cheval Blanc, Rosewood, Nikki Beach, shopping in Gustavia); St. Kitts & Nevis next; Antigua and the Hermitage Bay area; St. Lucia for Jade Mountain and the Sulphur Springs; St. Vincent for a private resort on Canouan. The Caribbean is at its best when you pick one island and stay.

Patagonia, Santiago, Atacama Desert — three very different Chiles. Patagonia was Senderos Lodge by helicopter, rafting and horseback into local farmhouses for breakfast. Cold plunges at camp, lamb roast from a guide's family farm. A real trip.

Santiago is a weekend: Ritz-Carlton, burgers at Ramblas, negronis at Siete. Valparaíso for the day. Then north to San Pedro de Atacama — geysers, salt flats, sky at elevation. One of the most quietly beautiful places we've been.
dining: Lamb roast at guide's farm, Patagonia
activities: Horseback to Las Escalas farm breakfast
Atacama Desert — geysers, salt flats, stargazing

prose: Two cities: Shanghai, for the city; Hangzhou, for the escape. We stayed at Amanyangyun outside Shanghai — camphor trees, white peacocks, the kind of hotel that makes jet lag feel like a deliberate choice. Day trips to Suzhou (canal city, forty minutes out) and Luzhi. Dinner at UltraViolet, which has one address and twenty-two courses and a different soundscape for each.

Hangzhou was the slow half. Amanfayun is a converted village at the foot of West Lake — walk to Faxi Temple, ring the bell, bow to the monks, take a calligraphy class in a language you don't speak. We ate tea-leaf eggs for breakfast on Hailey's birthday. We'd go back.
dining: UltraViolet by Paul Pairet (3 Michelin stars)
Mo Wang, Chengdu (Max's favorite casual restaurant)
activities: Day trip to Suzhou — ancient canal city
Luzhi boat ride with a singing boatwoman
Faxi Temple, Hangzhou — ring the bell

prose: Cartagena for four days at Sofitel Legend Santa Clara. Max sprained an ankle early; the hotel concierge sent a doctor to the room. Slowed us down, didn't stop us. El Barón for lunch. Cande for dinner (music, dancing, proper). Alquímico for the cocktails — number ten in the world at the time, easily.

Three stops: Poás, Monteverde, Arenal. Landed in San José, rented a car late, drove into a cloud forest. Our first hotel was part of a nature reserve — Rosti the dog followed us to our room. Fished trout off the wall for lunch the next day. Coffee tour at Doka Estate outside Poás, front to back.

Monteverde for the canopy walk and the Arenal volcano for the hot springs. Costa Rica does slow well. The food is quieter than Mexico's but the country is louder in every other way — birds, howler monkeys, rain on a tin roof.

Dubrovnik old city is an exhibit; stay outside the walls, walk in. Villa Dubrovnik has the rooftop view with Prosciutto & Wine Bar. Game of Thrones filming spots for the nerds. Restaurant 360 is the only Michelin star in the country and earns the view.
Boat day from the hotel through the Elaphiti islands. Cable car up for lunch at Panorama. Borders are fast on this side of the Balkans — Metkovic to Bosnia took minutes.

Limassol for the beach. Four Seasons (loosely affiliated, don't ask). Watersports from the hotel beach — kayaks, sailing, paddle board, jetskis. Run along the boardwalk. Tuesday's hotel-hosted Cyprus cultural event was surprisingly great — traditional tavli music, chicken souvlaki, a man running around with sixteen stacked glass cups on his head.
drinking: Beachfront cocktails at the Four Seasons
activities: Boardwalk run along the hotel strip

prose: Galápagos by Silversea — eight days, 544 nautical miles, 77–80°F every day. Started in Quito at JW Marriott with a walking tour and a quick look at Ciudad Mitad del Mundo (the equator), then flew Guayaquil to San Cristóbal and boarded the Silver Origin.

Highlights by island: Genovesa (Darwin Bay, Prince Phillip's Steps) for frigatebirds and red-footed boobies; Santa Cruz for giant tortoises; Isabela for flamingos and marine iguanas. Next time we do this on a private boat with two or three other couples.
activities: Genovesa Island — Darwin Bay, Prince Phillip's Steps

prose: Cairo, Luxor, and a Nile cruise — the classic route, and the right one. The Great Pyramids at Giza are more vertical than photos prepare you for; the stepped pyramid of Djoser at Saqqara is older; the Sphinx is smaller. We hitched a camel ride at the small pyramid's camel-parking lot and ate at the 9 Pyramids Lounge inside the park.

From Cairo we flew to Luxor, boarded the Farah Riverboat, and cruised the Nile stopping at Dayr al-Bahri (built for Queen Hatshepsut into a mountainside), the Valley of the Kings, Edfu, Kom Ombo, and Aswan. Egyptian cooking class on board. An alabaster stop between temples. It's the kind of trip that sounds contrived until you go.
hotels: Four Seasons Hotel Cairo at Nile Plaza
dining: Shawarma cooking class on the Farah
drinking: Lobby lounge at the Four Seasons Cairo
activities: Camel ride around the Giza pyramid field
Saqqara — stepped pyramid of Djoser, Teti
Nile cruise: Luxor → Edfu → Kom Ombo → Aswan

prose: One day in San Salvador. Checked Bitcoin Chivo ATM at Parque Morazán (El Salvador was the first country to adopt Bitcoin as legal tender). Hotel dinner then a Luis Miguel concert at Estadio Cuscatlán. Tacos Hermanos is one of the best lunch spots we’ve been to anywhere — the line gets long; get there early.
activities: Luis Miguel concert at Estadio Cuscatlán (if touring)

prose: Base at the Connaught in Mayfair, which has a bar that has been #1 in the world and was again the next year. London is a series of day trips from a good hotel: Windsor, Stonehenge, Heckfield Place, Cliveden, Bath. Fat Duck for dinner when you can get in. The Cotswolds over a weekend.

In the city itself: hop-on-hop-off for the first day, Buckingham Palace, Big Ben, Westminster Abbey, the Eye with the champagne ticket, Harrods because it's Harrods, men's grooming at Trumper's, Churchill War Rooms, British Museum. Hedonism Wines for the tasting. London rewards repetition; we keep coming back.
dining: The Fat Duck, Bray (3 Michelin stars)

prose: Tallinn's old town is the perfect size for three days. Schlosse Hotel in the middle of it. Mushroom soup and cheesecake at a restaurant we forgot the name of; cocktails at Whisper Sister (speakeasy); ramen the next day; Alexander Nevsky Cathedral, more impressive outside than in. Cocktails at Sigmund Freud. Italian at Contravento. Burgers at Grill ja Pruul. Dinner at Lee. Flew out via Helsinki to Iceland.

Tórshavn for three days. Hilton Garden Inn for the base. Ferry to Kalsoy for the Kallur Lighthouse hike and the Kópakonan (Seal Woman) statue. Sørvágsvatn (the lake that floats above the ocean), Múlafossur, Klæmintsgjógv sea cave. Go during Olavsøka (National Faroese Day) if you can time it.

Seaplane from Nadi to Turtle Island in the Yasawas. Carried off the plane at the dock, lunch on the sand in front of the bure, a Fijian choir at the path to announce cocktail hour. Bula dance-and-kava around a fire that first evening.

Boat to a neighboring island the next morning for diving — some of the best coral we’ve seen. Village kids from a nearby island came over that afternoon to sing songs and claim ice cream. Fiji does hospitality completely differently.
drinking: Kava with the Turtle Island staff

prose: Helsinki for a weekend. Hotel Kamp, walk through Senate Square past the Helsinki Cathedral, along Kanavaranta to the Uspensky Cathedral, and out to the Merisatama seafront. Löyly for a two-hour sauna session. Outdoor cinema at Kauppatori. Nokka for the tasting menu.

Paris first — the Ritz on Place Vendôme, dinner at Epicure, a nightcap at Little Red Door. Then Burgundy for the wine (and the cheese, and the other wine), the French Riviera for a week at Hotel du Cap-Eden-Roc, and Bordeaux when we wanted the left bank.
France is the country where we stop trying to see everything and just order another glass.
dining: L'Avant Comptoir de la Terre, Paris
La Tour d'Argent (1 Michelin star, oldest in Paris)
Le Train Bleu, Gare de Lyon (1 Michelin star)
drinking: Candelaria (taco shop up front, cocktail bar in back)
activities: Eiffel Tower at night from Trocadéro (twinkle on the hour)
Day trip through Meursault, Beaune & Dijon for wine

prose: Tbilisi for three days. Sheraton for the stay (though next time we’d stay closer in). Narikala Fortress by cable car, Mother of Georgia statue on the hill, European Square, the Tree of Life, Gabriadze clock tower puppet theatre. Drinks at 41 and Rude Boy. Gudauri ski resort is a day trip out.
hotels: Sheraton Grand Tbilisi (or closer in next time)

prose: Copenhagen in four days. Right in the middle of the action at D'Angleterre. Dinner at Noma and then dinner at Geranium, since they were the two best restaurants in the world at the time. Gasoline Grill for burgers at lunch. Torvehallerne food hall with schnapps tastings. A slice at Pizza Huset. Bike around the city. Tivoli at night.
activities: Copenhill (climb / ski on a power plant)

prose: Ten days: Athens, Crete, Mykonos, Santorini, Oia. Athens to start: Acropolis at opening, Panathenaic Stadium, dinner at US Embassy (the restaurant), drinks at The Clumsies (top-ranked bar, deservedly).

Island-hopping in a line: Santorini for Lauda in Oia and the Sigalas Vineyard tour; Mykonos for a speedboat day to ancient Delos and lunch at Nammos; Crete for the quiet. If you can only do three, do Crete, Santorini, Mykonos in that order.

Original flight from Reykjavík to Illulisat cancelled, rebooked the next morning, made it. Met Beda from Switzerland at the airport. Walked down to Disko Bay — UNESCO site, packed with icebergs — and then a four-hour boat ride (advertised as two) to camp at Saqqaq.

At camp you trade clothes for drysuits to do anything. Kayaking through ice fields in rain. Shipwreck site on a dawn walk. Sunset at 9:15pm, main tent at 10, northern lights that never came. One of the hardest trips to organize we've taken; one of the most different places we've ever been.

Antigua with a day trip to Lake Atitlán. El Convento Boutique Hotel for the stay, a 45-minute hike up Cerro de la Cruz for the view, a trip out to Tikal National Park for the jungle pyramids. Ulew is the speakeasy worth finding.
activities: Cerro de la Cruz hike (45 min)
Lake Atitlán walk through artisan markets

prose: Georgetown, one night. Marriott is the only real place to stay. We were advised not to leave the hotel after dark or without an escort, so we didn’t. Hard Rock Cafe tried to be our only dining option. A reminder that not every country is set up for visitors.
activities: Hotel stay (advisory: don't leave after dark)

prose: Kyoto first, then Tokyo. Kyoto is the Japan of your imagination — temples, moss, a bamboo grove you walk through in silence, a whiskey library at Suntory Yamazaki you have to reserve months out. We went to twelve temples and remember four of them clearly: Kinkaku-ji at opening, the rock garden at Ryōan-ji, the thousand stone Rakan at Otagi, and Fushimi Inari at sunset.

Tokyo was denser and louder and better than anywhere else we've been for food and drink. Toppan wagyu at Mouriya Gion, sushi at Sushidokoro Man, a magic show at L'Escamoteur where every cocktail comes with sleight of hand. Private maiko performance in Gion. If you can only do one country on the planet for a week, we'd vote for this one.
activities: Private maiko performance at Gion Miyagawacyo
Suntory Yamazaki whisky tasting (book months ahead)

prose: Roatán for the dive week. Kimpton on West Bay. Odyssey shipwreck dive was the highlight — a large wreck, good light through the reef. A night of bar-hopping in West End. Dinner at The Drunken Sailor (the pasta is good, promise).

Four Seasons Hong Kong is the anchor. Tram to Victoria Peak for the view, cable car to Ngong Ping in Lantau for the Big Buddha. Grand Hall of 10,000 Buddhas, Tai O fishing village, shopping on Ladies Street. Drinks at Coa (World's 50 Best), Quinary, Penicillin, Argo. Dinner at Wing, Carbone, and Caprice (3 stars) inside the hotel.
dining: Caprice at Four Seasons (3 Michelin stars)
activities: Cable car to Ngong Ping + Big Buddha

prose: Two trips: the Golden Circle and the South Coast. We flew in from Estonia and stopped on the drive to the hotel at the spot where the North American and European plates meet — you can step from one continent to the other. Nights at the Blue Lagoon, dinners of arctic char, the volcano tour where you ride a cable car down into the magma chamber of a dormant crater.
The second trip we based at Hotel Ránga on the south coast — horseback on Icelandic ponies, Skógafoss at dawn, a super‑jeep day into Þórsmörk. Weather never cooperates. That is the point of Iceland.

Kolkata is chaos with a pulse — flower markets under the Howrah Bridge, yellow taxis, couples having portrait sessions on the ghats, shaving ceremonies, the whole extended human condition happening before breakfast. Then Darjeeling: the three-hour drive up the mountain, the narrow-gauge railway that shares the road, a week of tea at the Glenburn Estate.
We've also been to Goa, Delhi, Agra, Jaipur. India rewards going slowly. Don't try to do all of it.
drinking: Hotel bar at the Oberoi Grand
activities: Hatkhola — where Hindu deities are crafted
Glenburn Tea Estate morning tea service

prose: Two trips: Dublin, then Dromoland Castle for the West. Dublin is Temple Bar at night (every night we had the energy), Jameson Distillery on Bow Street, Trinity College, a day trip to Belfast for the Titanic Museum and another to the Giant's Causeway with a lunch in Bushmills.

Dromoland is a proper castle stay — clay shooting, falconry, bike rides on the property, paddle boat on the pond, the walled garden. Cliffs of Moher thirty minutes away. Michelin-starred dinner at Earl of Thomond on site.
dining: Earl of Thomond at Dromoland (1 Michelin star)
activities: Bike ride around the Dromoland property

prose: We entered Israel by land from Jordan at the Yitzhak Rabin crossing. Our guide met us, and we started on the Jordan River at Yardenit for baptism, then lunch in Tiberias. Capernaum for Jesus's town, the Mount of Beatitudes, Nazareth and the Basilica of the Annunciation, Jerusalem by evening.

A full day on the Old City: Garden of Gethsemane, through the Muslim Quarter, along the Via Dolorosa by station, lunch between stations eight and nine, Church of the Holy Sepulchre, Mount Zion, King David's Tomb, the Western Wall. Next day: two hours at Yad Vashem, which isn't enough.
dining: Israeli lunch on Hayarden Street, Tiberias
drinking: Rooftop bar at the Mamilla Hotel
activities: Mount Zion, King David's Tomb, Upper Room
Yad Vashem (Holocaust Museum) — minimum half day

prose: We've been to Italy more times than any other country. Lake Como in late spring, Positano in high summer, Sicily in early fall, Sardinia when nobody else was there. We got married at Villa Pliniana on the lake, had breakfast on the Tremezzo terrace more mornings than we can count, and learned that the right table at Harry's Bar in Cernobbio is the one facing the water at seven.
If you only go once, make it the Amalfi Coast in June. If you go twice, add the lakes. If you go a third time, head south — Sicily and Sardinia are a different country altogether.
activities: Seaplane ride over Lake Como (depart from Como town)
Boat day on Lake Como — Villa Balbianello, Tremezzo
Boat day on the Amalfi coast, Positano to Capri
Vatican City & St. Peter's at opening hour

prose: Amman was for arrival and the best hummus of our lives (at the Foyer Lounge at the Four Seasons). Petra was the point. Four-hour drive south, stops at Mount Nebo, the Madaba Mosaic Map at St. George's, a mosaic workshop called Tree of Life, and a truck-stop lunch at New Jerusalem Rest House.

Petra itself is better than you expect. Walk in through the Siq, the rose-red sandstone opens up, and the Treasury reveals itself exactly like the movies said it would. We hiked to the Monastery, skipped the ride, got rushed out in what might have been an unofficial vehicle. Back to Amman for a shower.
dining: The Foyer Lounge, Four Seasons (best hummus)
activities: Madaba Mosaic Map at Greek Orthodox Church of St. George

prose: Yerevan for three days at Golden Palace Hotel. Northern Avenue has street musicians every evening. Ejmiatsin cathedral for Armenia’s spiritual center. Chimar for a proper sit-down; Tavern Yerevan for live music with reservation; Lavash for the last dinner. Phoenix for the nightcap.
activities: Northern Avenue walk with street musicians

prose: Astana and Almaty. Astana is newly-built — Right Embankment walk, Kenesary Khan monument, Bayterek Tower for the view. Lunch at Vechnoye Nebo in the Moskva Shopping Center (horse is the specialty).

Almaty for the mountains: cable car to Shymbulak ski resort, Russian Orthodox Ascension Cathedral, lunch at Qazaq Auyl (a fifteen-minute hike from Shymbulak, easily the most authentic meal of the trip).

Angama Mara sits on top of an escarpment with 360 degrees of the Maasai Mara below — the view that ends up in every safari brochure. Our first afternoon drive turned up a cheetah taking down an antelope from the first spot of tracking to the kill. Candle-lit BBQ in the woods that night.

Hot air balloon at six, breakfast on the grass when we landed, then honeymooning lions ten minutes into the drive back. More lions, more cheetahs, a hippo pool, a visit to the local women's craft center. Chapati means tortilla. Nzuri sana means delicious.
dining: Candle-lit BBQ dinner in the woods
Bush breakfast after hot air balloon
activities: Hot air balloon over the Mara River
Cheetah hunt — watched from start to kill

prose: Tarawa for two days at Motel Utirerei. ATM lines at the Parliament Building routinely exceed thirty people; locals bring plastic chairs and stake out a spot. Walked an hour down the main road Sunday morning past families heading to church. Saw Mormon missionaries coming and going at the motel.

Cheese sandwiches and fried rice for meals — food options are limited. Ran around sunset when it was no longer 90°F. One of the quietest countries we’ve been to, and one of the most vulnerable to rising seas. Go.

110-to-115 degrees the entire visit. Peace Palace (Al Salam Palace Museum) was empty except for our three guides; Kuwait Towers at 120 meters for the view and the call to prayer reverberating across the city; AlSadu Society for traditional Kuwaiti textile work; Americani Cultural Center (a former Christian missionary hospital). Avenues Mall is themed by neighborhood — Europe to Seoul in forty minutes. We caved and ate Chipotle.
drinking: Lobby lounge at the Four Seasons

prose: Karakol and the Tian Shan. Holy Trinity wooden cathedral, then a Dungan Mosque (Dungans: Chinese-origin Muslims with their own architecture). Drove to Broken Heart rocks and Seven Bulls mountains. Traditional Dungan dinner in a family home one night, Kyrgyz family lunch in Bokonbayevo the next day.

Eagle Hunter demonstration in the mountains — private show with a real eagle. Yurt stay at Ak Sai Travel on the shore of Issyk-Kul, one of the largest alpine lakes in the world. Cold nights, clear stars.
drinking: Kymyz (fermented mare's milk) at the yurt
activities: Broken Heart rocks + Seven Bulls mountains

prose: Luang Prabang from Amanpuri. Swim at Kuang Si waterfalls, elephant sanctuary, boat down the Mekong, climb Phou Si Hill for sunset over the river. The town itself is small and walkable. We ate every dinner at the hotel; next time, the cafes along the main street.

Rolled into Riga from Vilnius and went straight to dinner at Dilettante (Italian). Next morning, laundry at a laundromat; Space Falafel for lunch; a walk past the National Opera and the Ukraine embassy facing Russia's with Putin framed on the wall. Cocktails at Cloud Nine, pizza at Bella Napoli. Big Bag Bagels on the way out.

Vilnius is a walking city. Užupis for Italian on arrival, then spa session at the hotel, a tower climb for the old town view, burgers at a local spot, drinks at Apoteka, dinner at Dżiaugsmas ("happiness"), nightcap at Nomads Cocktail Bar.
Drive out includes Trakai Castle (5 euro parking in a farmer’s gravel lot) and the Hill of the Crosses — a literal hill in the countryside packed with thousands of them. An hour later: Latvia.
Drove in from Champagne. Walked Edouard André Park, took the Pfaffenthal Lift down to the Grund. Drinks at Shinzo. Next morning: laundry, a quick stop at Five Guys, and off to Brussels.
activities: Pfaffenthal Lift down to the Grund

prose: Two nights in Skopje, one at Lake Ohrid. Skopje is sculpture-forward — every square has five statues. We walked Porta Macedonia, Karposovo Square, then Citjka (the Turkish bazaar) for lunch. Speakeasy (the actual name of the bar) for cocktails, Van Gogh after, then dinner in Debar Maalo.

Matka Canyon is a twenty-minute drive out and worth the detour. Europe kayak competition was going on when we showed up; boat to the cave, wine and cheese fondue at the lone restaurant. Lake Ohrid for the closing night — a walk out to Golden Beach Park at dawn, and then Albania.
dining: Bela Voda Tikves wine & cheese fondue, Matka Canyon
activities: Lake Ohrid walk to Golden Beach Park

prose: Kuala Lumpur weekend. Four Seasons for the base. Thean Hou Temple for the fortune, Batu Caves for the colored stairs up to the cave (monkeys included), Petaling Street for copies of whatever you want. Dinner at Nobu in the Four Seasons. Drinks at Bar Trigona (top 50 in Asia).

Three days. Hotel Xara Palace in Rabat for the base. Catamaran out of Marina Kalkara through Valletta Harbour into the Mediterranean — anchored for drinks, swimming, late lunch on deck. Local Maltese wine (Ta’ Betta) at L'Enoteca. Flew to Paris next.
activities: Catamaran from Marina Kalkara through Valletta Harbour

prose: Majuro first, then boat to Bikendrik Island. Suzy, the Austrian owner, met us at the dock and walked us to the rooms. One villa had an Andy Warhol sketch of a revolver on the wall. Aperitivo before dinner, beef bourguignon, digestif on the porch while Suzy chain-smoked and told stories about moving here.
Dive the Aquarium — just outside the reef where the ocean hits the atoll. Good viz, a lot of life. The kind of island you only get to if you’re trying. We’re trying.
drinking: Digestif on the porch with Suzy

prose: Constance Le Prince Maurice for a quiet few days on the east coast. Dinner one night at the sister Constance hotel's steakhouse — incredible wine list. Beach, spa, repeat.

Mexico City is the best food city in the Americas — we'll die on that hill. Pujol for dinner, Brujas for the cocktail, Pizza Roma for the late-night slice we kept going back to. Day of the Dead turns the city into an open-air altar: marigolds lining Calle Chapultepec, face paint on every corner, the whole place glowing.

Trip 1 was Mexico City, Teotihuacán, Coyoacán. Trip 2 was the beach side — Tulum, Oaxaca. The speakeasies just keep getting better: Hanky Panky has a passport menu, Licorería Limantour is routinely the best bar in Latin America. Brujas is the one we keep thinking about.
activities: Coyoacán wander — Mercado & Frida Kahlo house
Day of the Dead on Calle Chapultepec

prose: Bobwiki on Air Nauru, flight ON24. Chinese food for dinner at Praises (vegetable chop suey), past pit bulls chained to trees and kids swimming in the ocean. Island circumference walk the next morning — 3 hours, 82°F and 74% humidity.

Tuna sashimi and cheese pizza for dinner with Tony from New Zealand, who we met watching locals practice rugby at sundown. Nauru: the world’s third-smallest country by area, and one of the most underrated remote stops on the Pacific route.

Pohnpei by way of Palau on Nauru Airlines. Hiked up to a lookout in the rain. Takeout pizza for dinner on a plastic patio table. Next morning: fried rice takeout and an hour's drive to Nan Madol, the ruins of a floating city built in the lagoon in the 13th century. Shoes off to cross the canal.

Entered by land from Albania at the Muriquan crossing, quick as anywhere in the Balkans. North along the coast: Sveti Stefan (Aman-adjacent) for twenty minutes, Budva old town for another twenty — narrow hallways like Venice. Then finally up to One&Only Portonovi, a two-year-old hotel with excess on tap.

Riva boat day from the Tapasake marina: submarine tunnel (a real thing), Our Lady of the Rocks, the bay reminding us of Lake Como from every angle. Skipper's lunch pick at Hotel Forza Terra, then docking in Kotor to walk the old city and crane necks at the wall on the ridge.

Marrakech does a thing where it convinces you, within a day, that you live there. We stayed at Royal Mansour — riads with their own private courtyards, a spa where the hammam ritual feels like getting a second skin. Mornings in the medina, afternoons in the Jardin Majorelle, evenings at La Grande Table Marocaine.

One evening we drove into the Agafay Desert for dinner under the stars: Berber tents, musicians, a fire show, and the odd quiet of driving back through true darkness. We flew out through Casablanca — worth an overnight, not much more.
dining: La Grande Table Marocaine, Royal Mansour
Berber dinner in the Agafay Desert
activities: Agafay Desert evening with Berber music & fire show

prose: Benguerra Island at &Beyond. Two kinds of nights: dive days and swim nights — bioluminescence in the ocean after dark, stars over the lodge clear enough to spot shooting ones within minutes. The Bazaruto Archipelago is as under-visited as Africa's coast gets.

Kathmandu is chaos in the best way — prayer flags, monkeys, traffic that moves like water. We spent a few days in the city, then chartered a helicopter to see the eight highest peaks in a single morning: Everest, Lhotse, and six more. We landed at Lukla for breakfast, stopped at a monastery where red‑hat Tibetans were in ceremony, and flew on to Jomsom and Lo Manthang — the walled city in Upper Mustang, near the Tibet border.

From Jomsom we trekked to Annapurna base camp, then took a chopper to Pokhara and stayed at the Fishtail Lodge on the lake. A slower end to a fast trip. We'd go back for Everest Base Camp — and eventually did, in 2026.
dining: Dinner with a cultural show, Kathmandu
Local kitchen at the Royal Mustang
activities: Helicopter tour of the eight highest peaks (Everest, Lhotse + 6)
Upper Mustang walled city, Lo Manthang

prose: Three stops: Auckland and Waiheke Island for the wine; Hobbiton because we're nerds and fine with it; Queenstown for Christmas. Santa Claus met us at the Waiheke ferry dock playing carols on a trumpet, from a dinghy. We drank our way through Mudbrick, Man O' War, and Waiheke Distilling Co. over one afternoon.

Queenstown for winter: Fergburger (the line looks bad, it moves), Christmas Eve at The Bunker, Amisfeld wine tastings. In New Zealand a bar must also serve food by law — which means the food comes last and lukewarm. Worth knowing.
dining: Man O' War vineyard lunch, Waiheke
activities: Ferry to Waiheke — Santa Claus on a dinghy
One Tree Hill & Auckland Winter Gardens

prose: Managua in three hours on the way to the airport. Intercontinental as base. Guide didn’t speak English but we got it done: Managua Cathedral, Monumento a Sandino, Rotonda Hugo Chavez, Campana de la Paz, Palacio Nacional and Plaza de la Revolución, Volcán Masaya. Intercontinental restaurants were actually good.
activities: Palacio Nacional + Plaza de la Revolución

prose: Three trips. Oslo first for Maaemo (Norway's only three-star at the time, and the restaurant that put Scandinavian dining on the map) and Himkok, one of the best cocktail bars in the world. Then back in winter for northern lights near Tromsø. Then Bergen — ferry through the Sognefjord, Tvindefossen waterfall, the village of Undredal, the Flåmsbana railway.

Lahore first — Sajjad met us at the airport with marigold and jasmine garlands. The roads into the Pearl Continental were noticeably more secured than we expected. Lahore Fort, Badshahi Mosque, Shalmi Market — live chickens in coops, doctors practicing in market stalls. Haveli Restaurant for dinner with a perfect view over the mosque.
Then Islamabad, Taxila, Peshawar, Rawalpindi. Pakistan is not on the well-worn tourist track — the people we met were among the most hospitable we’ve encountered anywhere.
drinking: Hotel lounge at the Pearl Continental

prose: Koror for a dive trip. Eight hours from Honolulu to Guam plus a short connection. Got three dives in on day one because of jet lag: inner reef with a grouper, eels, black-and-white tips. Then the Milky Way — a limestone mud lagoon where guides dive down with snorkels and come up with white clay for a face mask.

Lunch on a small beach island with Japanese and Chinese picnickers. Frogs came out at night and hopped out of our way on the property. Palau is the kind of place that earns repeat dive trips — Blue Corner, Blue Holes, Jellyfish Lake.

One night in Panama City between Galápagos and Uruguay. Dinner at Makoto (hot), lunch at Fonda Lo Que Hay (also hot, local tip), old town wander, Panama Canal at the Miraflores Locks — the tourist thing to do, and correctly so. Dinner at Azahar before the red-eye out.

Tufi Dive Resort, a small village on the eastern coast. Dinner starts with a bowl of popcorn. Morning dive at Honeymoon with Andy (local guide) — an hour speedboat ride out, a pod of 50+ dolphins escorting us back. Grilled fish, potato fries, cucumber salad for lunch.

Butterfly reserve the next day with Yankee — Queen Alexandra Birdwing, the largest butterfly in the world. Glowing pink-and-purple sunsets, popcorn with coconut, fish soup every night. One of the least-visited countries and one of the easiest to love.

Lima at Belmond Miraflores. Central for the tasting menu (top in the world the year we went), La Mar for the ceviche, Maido and Rafael for more proof that Lima is a food destination. Carnaval for the cocktail. We did the Amazon separately from Iquitos. Machu Picchu the trip before this.

Cebu and Palawan. Cebu at Shangri-La for the dive week — the sardine run in formation, blowfish, sea turtles. Then flight to Palawan: ninety-minute drive past butcher shacks and stray dogs, caves at Ugong Rock with zip lines, the Puerto Princesa underground river. Lunch at Cacaoyan Forest Park.

Warsaw was a waypoint, not a destination — our base for driving into Ukraine. Hotel Bristol for the night, a highly-rated dinner nearby, 8:10am departure for the border. Came back from Lviv with a two-hour border crossing and ordered hotel room service. Flew to Sofia the next day. Poland deserves more time than we gave it.
dining: Top-rated Warsaw dinner near the hotel

prose: Lisbon first, then Sintra, then Cascais. The Tram 28 line is famously long; we took a tuk-tuk and got a better tour for it. São Vicente was the best view in the city; the Church of St. Anthony was the closest we came to quiet. Bairro Alto for dinner (Cervejaria Ramiro-adjacent), Red Frog to close the night, and the Time Out Market for lunch.

Sintra is a day trip — Pena Palace, Quinta da Regaleira's initiation well, the eucalyptus drive to Colares. Cascais for the coast and the oysters. Pink Street at night is a small stretch that has convinced itself it's a larger scene.
activities: Church of St. Anthony, Santa Maria Maior

prose: One day in Doha with Habib, a Qatari local in formal linens. Souk for the Falcon Market — live falcons for sale, every stall — and the ceremonial changing of the guard at the main government building, twice a day. Qatari Museum was between exhibitions. Islamic Museum was the full history across four floors. FIFA World Cup statue, dhow photos, skyline photos. Qatari breakfast, then Abu Dhabi.
dining: Qatari breakfast at the Four Seasons
drinking: Hotel lounge at Four Seasons Doha
activities: Doha city tour with a local guide

prose: Kigali is the most orderly city in Africa — no plastic bags, streets that get swept. Bridge, our guide, walked us through the Genocide Museum and shared that he was seventeen during the hundred days. It reframed the rest of the trip. Lunch at a local place instead of at Mille Collines.

Volcanoes National Park is three hours north. Hotel Sabyinyo — tea at five thirty in the morning on the patio, then the trek. Our guide Eugene paired us with two other couples, and we met a family of sixteen gorillas: one silverback (Putura), eight juveniles, a two-month-old baby. The second day's trek was easier — mostly flat, through farmland.
activities: 25-person welcome back from a gorilla trek

prose: Taumeasina, landed from Vanuatu. Dinner at Paddles (Niçoise salad and ravioli). Morning training session with Etu, our Samoan-born, USA-trained coach, who cracked us both left-and-right. 2am departure for the 5am flight to Tuvalu — short stop, warm memory.

Glasgow first for the whisky (Arran 10, GlenDronach 12, Oban) at Pot Still, pizza at Paesano, a round of darts at Flight Club. Then Edinburgh, a night at the Balmoral, and boarding the Royal Scotsman — Belmond's train across the Highlands. Bagpipes as we walked onto the platform.

The Scotsman is the point. Bar cart for cocktails, live accordion music, Dior facial on board, stops at Inverness and Plockton (a tiny lakeside village). Max's kilt night. Four days, four or five stops, a lot of whisky. The best way to see the Highlands without driving.
dining: Bar cart dining on the Royal Scotsman
activities: Royal Scotsman train through the Highlands

prose: Drove in from Sarajevo to Belgrade — 260km, four hours, a border crossing that took fifteen minutes. Belgrade surprised us: parts reminded us of Buenos Aires, the rest of Brooklyn. Nikola Tesla Museum, Orthodox Cathedral of Saint Sava, Belgrade Fortress. Square Nine Hotel spa for the morning after Riddle.

Iva New Balkan Cuisine for the Michelin-recommended lunch, Giovanni's Pizzeria for the Tuesday-night dinner with a free tiramisu. Packed with independent shops. Belgrade does a lot with not much — and the bar scene runs late.

Félicité and La Digue. Six Senses Zil Pasyon is the anchor — villas in the granite boulders, private beaches, a spa cut into the rock. Day trip by boat to La Digue for Anse Source d'Argent (the most photographed beach in the world, for reasons). North Island next time.

We've never been in a city more committed to being good at being a city. Fullerton Bay for the first few nights, Mandarin Oriental for the view, Marina Bay Sands for the infinity pool that does feature in every Instagram. Chinatown for the Buddha Tooth Temple, Haji Street for the perfume shop, Little India for the afternoon you suddenly need, Mount Faber for the three-sixty.

The bar scene is maybe the best in Asia. Jigger & Pony, Analogue, 28 HongKong Street, MO Bar, Manhattan at the Conrad, Atlas at Parkview, No Sleep Club for last call. Les Amis for three-star dinner. Raffles High Tea for the ritual. And the private perfume class at Sifr Aromatics.
activities: Light to Night projection at National Gallery

prose: Bratislava for a night. Drove in from Lake Bled through Austria, briefly escorted by Hells Angels (a genuinely-used phrase in this trip). Classic car show in Hviezdoslav Square on arrival. Drinks at Antique American Bar (maybe ten of them). Mirror Bar at the Radisson Blu Carlton for sunset. Two-hour drive to Bucharest in the morning.

Ljubljana is compact and walkable — Dragon Bridge, Tromostovje, a hike up to Ljubljana Castle through St. Nicholas's iron doors, wine tasting at Granjska Vinoteca on the way. Local street food from Chutys and Dobra. A celebration broke out downtown while we were at the castle; we went down to see what was going on.

Then Lake Bled. Row boat out to Blejski Otok, climb the clock tower for the quarter-hour chime, ring the wishing bell inside. A 4.5-mile run around the whole lake the next morning. Mega Burger for the reward. Vintgar Gorge an hour away if you have the morning. Slovenia is the easy yes.
activities: Clock tower + wishing bell at the island church

prose: Honiara for two nights at Heritage Park. Gym, breakfast, walk to the local market where a young man was playing guitar and singing church hymns. Penne arrabbiata and beef noodle soup for lunch. Short, quiet stop. One of the hardest countries to get to and one of the calmest.

Three stops: Cape Town, Franschhoek, and Sabi Sand. Cape Town peninsula drive — Camps Bay, the Cape of Good Hope, the road reputedly the most beautiful in the world. Table Mountain by cable car. Dinner at FYN, drinks at The House of Machines and Asoka.

Franschhoek is the South African winelands — the wine train loops by stops at Morgenster (wine and olive oil), Vergelegen, Waterford. Then a flight to Sabi Sand for Big Five in a day: a male lion with six cubs, a leopard resting in a tree, elephants browsing past the Land Cruiser's fender.
activities: Cape Peninsula drive — Camps Bay, Chapman's Peak
Sabi Sand safari — Big Five in a day

prose: Download Naver and Kakao before landing — Google Maps and Uber don't work. Four Seasons Seoul for the base, Sulwhasoo Spa for the morning, and then the bar circuit: Cham Bar, Zest, Les Chambre (NYC speakeasy, live music), Alice (themed to Alice in Wonderland). Korean BBQ at Woorahman. Jueun for the dinner we keep thinking about.

The DMZ is worth the early start. Two South Korean guards boarded our bus to check passports, we swapped to a secured bus, rode a monorail down into the Third Infiltration Tunnel, and then looked across into North Korea through binoculars. It felt like a dispatch from a different time zone. Kwangjang Market, Gwanghwamun — on separate days.
activities: DMZ tour — Third Infiltration Tunnel + North Korea overlook

prose: Madrid, Barcelona, San Sebastián, Ibiza, Mallorca — we've put in the hours. Madrid is the Spain of tapas, late dinners at A Barra, churros at San Ginés. Salmon Guru for the cocktail. Retiro Park on a Sunday.

San Sebastián is the best place to eat in Europe — three three-star restaurants inside twenty kilometers (Arzak, Akelarre, Mugaritz) and pintxos bars in between. Barcelona is Gaudí and the Boqueria. Ibiza is for the summer you’re ready to lose. Mallorca is the Spain you go back to in your forties.
dining: Pintxos crawl — La Cepa, Bár Nestor

prose: Galle for the colonial walls, the gemstone shops, the turtle sanctuary with an albino one, and a tea plantation tour where we learned about Virgin White Tea — an ancient Chinese ritual. Hot weather, but the city walks itself.

The real reason to come is the helicopter to Sigiriya. Rainy morning, some back-and-forth with weather, and then up we went — a quick fifteen-minute climb to the top of Lion Rock, a fly-around of Buddhist temples, and back to the hotel by lunch. Massages at the hotel. Next stop: the Maldives.
activities: Tea plantation tour — Virgin White Tea
Lion Rock climb (15 mins for the quick, 45 mins average)

prose: Paramaribo at Royal Torarica. Arrived 3am after a delayed flight from Guyana, two-hour drive from the airport. Walked the old Dutch colonial streets, stopped at the Presidential Palace and the Saint-Peter-and-Paul Basilica. Palmtree Garden for the photo. Roline’s de Waag for a good lunch.

Stockholm in a long weekend. Grand Hotel at the harbour, scooter around the islands, a look inside Hellman's Music Shop. Pizza at 800 Grader. Cocktails at Gemma — hot and lively — and Lucy's Flower Shop. The archipelago is the right thing to do with a full day.

Three days in Taipei. Mandarin Oriental as base. Lungshan Temple (one of the oldest in the city), Chiang Kai-shek Memorial Hall, Shilin Night Market for the stuffed animals and the prawn fishing. Din Tai Fung at 101 Tower for the ritual. Street foot massage somewhere in between.

Mnemba Island is the kind of place that exists just off Zanzibar and stays mostly unspoken. Thirty-acres, six villas at the time, one beach walk that takes you around the entire circumference. Fish tacos for lunch the first day, and a nest of green sea turtles hatched at sunset. We scuba dove off the reef twice. The second dive went to twenty meters.

We also did Ngorongoro Crater and the Serengeti on a separate trip — the Great Migration in the calving season, with lions waiting inside the acacia trees for the wildebeest to get close. Forty antelope follow every sunrise on Mnemba. They look like baby Bambis walking around the lawn.
activities: Scuba diving off Mnemba Island reef
Hot air balloon over the Serengeti

prose: Ko Samui for the beach week, Bangkok for the city days. We stayed at the Four Seasons on Ko Samui, which is the kind of place that fills the gap between vacation and retirement. One morning we went out on a snorkel boat, got caught in weather, ended up at a secret beach off Ko Pha-ngan called Ko Raham. A private dinner on the sand that night. The next day, elephants.

Bangkok was all bars. The Bamboo Bar at the Mandarin Oriental felt like stepping into a novel. Asia Today, Teens of Thailand, the BKK Social Club at the Four Seasons — all world‑class, all different. Chinatown on the night we arrived, Chinese New Year packed into every alley.
dining: Four Seasons Thai restaurant, Ko Samui
Beach BBQ dinner, Four Seasons Ko Samui
activities: Snorkel day trip — Ko Pha-ngan, Ko Raham secret beach
Private dinner on the beach, Ko Samui
Chinese New Year in Yaowarat, Bangkok

prose: Palm Springs Hotel in Dili. A film crew was setting up in the lobby when we checked in — Timor-Leste is barely a tourism destination, which is most of the appeal. Walked the waterfront, climbed Cristo Rei (the Christ statue at the end of the peninsula). Small country; one day is enough.

Swimming with humpback whales is the only reason to come — and the only reason you need. Book multiple days because weather won't always cooperate. Waterfront Lodge is the local family-style stay; Tanoa is the corporate option with the pool. Little Italy for dinner when you're tired of fish.
activities: Swimming with humpback whales (book multiple days)

prose: Cappadocia first: the hot-air balloon ride at dawn is the cliche, and the cliche is right. Rising with three hundred other balloons over a landscape of fairy chimneys, watching the sun come up — the kind of thing that sticks. Below, the underground cities at Özkonak, the rock churches, the castle at Uçhisar at sunset.

Istanbul next: the Four Seasons there is a converted prison (the building, not the service). Hagia Sophia at first light, Topkapi Palace after lunch, a boat across the Bosphorus to Park Fora for dinner. Istanbul traffic is the worst in the world — give it an hour more than you think.
drinking: Hotel bar at Four Seasons Sultanahmet
activities: Hot-air balloon ride at dawn over Cappadocia
Turkish hammam at Four Seasons spa

prose: The Gates of Hell is why we came: a collapsed Soviet drilling rig that's been burning for fifty years in the Karakum Desert. We camped in yurts at the Owadan Tourism site, ate dinner, met a baby hedgehog, drank whiskey, watched the flames for hours.

Ashgabat the day after — all cars are white (a law), all buildings white marble, roads wide enough for military parades. Paytagt rooftop for dinner. The Wedding Palace was packed for a Tuesday. This is the strangest country we've visited.
drinking: Whiskey at the yurt camp fireside

prose: Funafuti atoll — 2am wake-up in Fiji to catch the 5am flight, arrival mid-morning. The hotel had one common area. The airstrip becomes a playground every evening when a flight isn’t due in. Half-marathon training run on the tarmac at dawn. Cheese sandwiches three meals in a row.

Walked to both ends of the island on successive days. Motor-biked around on day three. Kids on the runway on Saturday morning playing soccer. The country is 26 square kilometers and nine atolls, the highest point four meters above sea level.
activities: Walk to each end of the atoll

prose: Four stops: Entebbe for the first night, Kisoro for the gorilla trek, Kibale for the chimpanzees, Jinja on the Nile. Gorilla trek morning was an hour-and-a-half down through a tea field, across streams, past local farmers' plots, and then an hour in the presence of the family — a silverback and a dozen juveniles — before the ninety-percent-uphill climb back.

Chimps are faster and louder. The walking tour through Kibale passed kids kicking a ball in a field and women working cassava. We ended at Lemala Wildwaters on an island in the White Nile, which is the best lodge we stayed at in Uganda.
dining: Posho, matoke, rice & beans — traditional Ugandan lunch
activities: Gorilla trek in Bwindi Impenetrable Forest
Chimp trek at Kibale National Park

prose: Drove from Warsaw to Lviv — four hours to the border, another to town. We were there a few days. 5am sirens the first night; the hotel directed us to the basement bomb shelter. Back to bed, then tulips in Stryi Park at noon, lunch at Kryivka (a bunker-themed restaurant with wartime propaganda on the walls).

Meat and Justice for dinner (dungeon-and-torture-themed), 12 Steps Bar for the real cocktails. A drive out to Drohobych for the countryside cottage, then back into Poland at Medyka. The Ukrainians we met were incredibly kind. We want to go back when the war ends.

Dubai at its own scale: the Burj Khalifa at eleven at night with no one in line, an aquarium inside a mall, a deep-dive pool with a submerged pool table at the bottom. Scalini for caviar pasta. Amazonico for the long lunch. Ossiano at Atlantis for the floor-to-ceiling aquarium dining.

Bvlgari Bar for cocktails, Aura Skypool on top of the St. Regis for the afternoon view. Deep Dive Dubai: the world's deepest pool at 60 meters, chess and pool photos on the way down. Dubai is exactly what it advertises.
activities: Dubai Fountain show at the Burj
Dubai Mall — aquarium + Chinatown + Play Dubai

prose: José Ignacio, not Punta del Este proper. Playa Vik for the base — quiet beach, massages at "The Shack" at Bahía Vik next door, sunset cocktails at the fire pit. Nothing opens before noon.

Restaurant at Garzón is worth the 20-minute drive — Francis Mallmann's wood-fired place, and a short trip from the quiet coast we'd been missing. Come in December or January if you want the scene; we were earlier and loved it that way.
activities: "The Shack" massage & yoga at Bahía Vik

prose: Tashkent, Samarkand, Bukhara, Khiva — the Silk Road as it's advertised. Broadway (Sayilgoh) in Tashkent was unexpectedly lively, Pepsi Music Festival going on the night we arrived. Le Bouton threw us an anniversary surprise with heart-shaped pizza. Independence Square was half-closed for security.

Train from Tashkent to Samarkand, then driving onward. Samarkand is the Registan. Bukhara is for wandering the madrasas. Khiva is a walled city where time mostly paused — and the last stop before crossing into Turkmenistan.

Port Vila — country 139 at the time. Horseback ride through the hills, kava with the locals the first night, a drive past Rainbow City (either a construction project or an abandonment, unclear). Pacific pass-through, not a destination. Good bones.

Saigon is a late city — it runs on motorbike rivers and alley bars. The Park Hyatt is in the middle of it. Pizza 4P's for a familiar lunch, Stir hidden at the end of an alleyway for the cocktail, Summer Experiment for the next one, and Luu Bar in a hipster district that could pass for Venice Beach. The Cu Chi Tunnels are worth the 1.5-hour drive; the War Remnants Museum is harder but worth more.

From Saigon we took a day into the Mekong Delta — boat to a coconut factory, a candy shop where they shuck the shell in front of you, and a bamboo-mat weaver's house that looked like every other house until you walked in. Vietnamese water puppetry for the last night.
activities: Mekong Delta day — coconut factory, candy shop, bamboo-mat weaver

prose: Two camps: Toka Leya on the Zambezi for Victoria Falls from the Zambian side, then Chinzombo in South Luangwa for the walking safari we'd come for. Boat cruise with Godfrey through hippo pods on our first evening; church service in English-then-Chichewa on Sunday morning; elephants overtook camp while we were out.

Luangwa game drives turned up hyenas, zebras, impalas, giraffes, crocs. A ninety-minute walking safari past a herd of elephants on the way back to the boat. Sundowners on the river every night — Jack Daniels for him, gin and tonic for her — while the light went.
dining: Bush breakfast after a dawn drive
drinking: Sundowners on the Zambezi — Jack Daniels & G&T

prose: Victoria Falls on the Zimbabwean side is bigger, wetter, and closer. You can see the bridge where the country meets Zambia — people were bungee-jumping and zip-lining, we were not. Sunset cruise on the Zambezi: crocs, hippos, storks. Next morning we flew the Cessna over the falls en route to Botswana. Short stop; worth it.
drinking: Sunset cruise cocktails on the Zambezi

prose: Flew in from Baku, picked up a rental in Barcelona, then drove three hours up into the Pyrenees. The main town runs along a river lined with art installations — a good way to stretch your legs after the drive before checking in for the spa.

One full day was enough. Gondola up to Grandvalira, cheese fondue at altitude, people putting on a golf course where the snow usually is. On the way out, we drove to Valencia and stopped at a Spanish gas station for bocadillos de jamón — better than most lunches we paid a table for.
activities: Art walk along the river through the main town
Winter and summer sports at the resort

Well, we’ve finally reached our 100th country in February. It feels like it took forever to get here. At the same time, it feels like a gigantic accomplishment, and a certain weight has been lifted. Something about hitting that 100 mark makes you feel like you’ve actually done it. A little pat on the back. Only 65 more countries to go! 🫠

Countries 76-100 was a mixed bag of travel, from finishing out Central Asia (“the Stans”) to the world of India, a tropical few weeks in The Caribbean, exploring the ins and outs of Central America and finally making it to the larger-than-life China. We’ve officially had food poisoning in all continents, visited 7 of 7 world wonders, Hailey became travel fluent in Japanese (ok, she knows 10 words), and Max’s Spanish is at an impressive 30% fluent!

A common question we get is to share our favorite cities, hotels, countries and experiences. From the last round of travel, here’s how things stack up Favorite Experiences from countries 76-100 (in order of preference): Visiting the Registan in Samarkand, Uzbekistan Walking around the streets of Khiva, Uzbekistan Seeing the Door to Hell in Turkmenistan - this is off the beaten path!

Cruising down the Mekong River in Luang Prabang, Laos Sharing our breakfast with monks in Laos as they make their daily trek to their temples in Luang Prabang, Laos Taking the Belmond train through the Malaysian jungle Taking Rickshaws around Georgetown Penang, Malaysia Seeing Mt. Everest via helicopter, setting flags up at at Annapurna Base Camp & hiking around Lo Mutang, Nepal - the antiquity of the town, it’s people, and their ways will leave you breathless.

Going to Zhong’s & Temples on Buddha’s reincarnation day in Bhutan Climbing up to Tigers Nest in Bhutan Walking through the markets in Delhi Tiger safari in India, seeing 7 tigers, mother tiger with her 4 cubs (and also a few black bears)

Hiking to the top of Sigiriya Rock in Sri Lanka Diving in the Maldives (seeing a pregnant sting ray!) Diving around the Underground Sculpture Park in Grenada Diving shipwrecks in Roatan, Honduras The Impression Lijiang show in Lijiang Bana experience in the Shanghai, Spa Spending a month in china at 6 different cities - seeing it’s true diversity Seeing the pandas in Chengdu, China
Spending 2 hours climbing the Great Wall of china Favorite Hotels from countries 76-100 (in order of preference): Cheval Blanc: Maldives (perhaps top 5 of all time)
Amarvilas in Agra, India: aesthetically one of the most beautiful hotels we’ve stayed at Mandarin Oriental Taipei, Taiwan
Kimpton: Roatan, Honduras Favorite Dining Experiences from countries 76-100 (in order of preference):
Peruvian Bhutanese - Chili & cheese in Bhutan!!!
Chinese if it’s at Mo Wang
Diving with swarms of fish in Cebu, Phillipines Favorite Countries (from country 76-100)

Max and I have just wrapped up 50 countries together and are amazed at how quickly our trip is flying (pun intended) by. We are just a little over one year into our travels, and so far this year we’ve already taken 37 flights, and logged 53,000 miles. That’s over two trips around the world. Our 50th country ended up being an unlikely, unsuspected, and unplanned travel destination: Ukraine. We are now on a 3-week road trip through the Baltics and the Balkans. Greetings from Bulgaria!

We miss all our friends and family back home and hope that you might take the opportunity to come meet us on part of our travels. Or, at the least, share any travel tips you have for us in our upcoming countries and cities. See where we’re headed next here.

Most people want to know what our favorites are. We’ve jotted down a quick list to share our favorite places, hotels, foods, and excursions; at least up until now and in no particular order. The list continues to evolve with the more ground that we cover, and we’d be remiss if we didn’t also make sure it’s clear that no country quite compares to The United States of America 🇺🇸

Our favorite hotels: Il San Pietro in Positano, Italy - for the impeccable Italian style and ‘cool’ relaxed aesthetic Hotel du cap Eden Roc in Antibes, France - for the manicured perfection mixed with luxurious ocean views Aman Tokyo in Tokyo, Japan - for the open and minimalistic design paired with flawless service Marbella Club in Marbella, Spain - for the perfect balance of upscale beach bungalow relaxation and evenings full of swanky entertainment Akelarre in San Sebastian, Spain - for the unique and clean design paired with breathtaking views overlooking San Sebastián Heckfield Place in London, England - for the perfect city escape to get a taste of the true English countryside experience Villa Pliniana in Lake Como, Italy - for many reasons, and also because we got married here :)

Italy - no surprise here Japan - their commitment to perfection is unwavering Greece - fresh, clean, delicious, plentiful Turkiye - wide range of flavors that all mesh together harmoniously Switzerland - everything so fresh, farm-to-table-like
Isreal - kosher and cleanliness is unbeatable

Our 50th country ended up being an unlikely, unsuspected, and unplanned travel destination: Ukraine. We are now on a three-week road trip through the Baltics and the Balkans. Max and I celebrated our 1 year wedding anniversary in Uzbekistan with a casual pizza dinner and an early night in at the hotel. Every day feels like an anniversary when you live on the road. Somebody we met during our travels joked that we celebrated our first anniversary before our honeymoon was over.

Since the last time we wrote, we’ve knocked off 25 more countries; reaching a total of 75 countries traveled together since March of 2022. Here’s a quick recap of the last chunk of travel: After our 5-week car ride through The Balkans, we took a quick trip on the historic Orient Express Train with YPO International before embarking on a 5-week trip through Africa.

Those of you that have been to Africa know how it can change you and you’ll leave the African Safari experience a changed person. Spending 5 weeks with Mother Nature, watching all various stages of the circle of life pass before your every eyes is a sobering and beautiful experience.

After a beautiful and blissful tour of Africa, we went back up to Europe to run through a future YPO planning trip, met with friends who were vacationing in Europe for the summer, and then we flew up to Northern Europe to start exploring those countries that neither of us had been to before. Going forward, all countries that we visit will be new for the both of us.

We crossed off the Caucasus Countries, made an impromptu stop in Dubai, and just wrapped up 21 days in Central Asia visiting “The Stans” (all but Afghanistan and Pakistan). From the last round of travel, here’s how things stack up: Our Favorite Hotels (and/or safari camps):
Aman Montenegro Angama Mara in the Maasai Mara, Kenya
Singita Ebony in Sabi Sand, South Africa DumaTau in Linyanti Concession, Botswana
Casa Mari Luigia, Modena - perhaps one of the most unique hotel & dining combo experiences we’ve ever had
Franschhoek, South Africa San Sebastián, Spain (ok, maybe not a “hidden gem”, but still deserving of a mention)

Experience Highlights: Traveling (and sleeping) on the Orient Express Train from Budapest to Bucharest Every single safari experience in the various camps in Botswana Watching hundreds of baby turtles hatch from their nest and embark on their maiden voyage to the ocean in Mnemba Island, Zanzibar Spending hours observing silverback gorilla families in the jungle in Rwanda Cruising through the thin canals of Burgundy on a renovated private barge Getting drenched at Victoria Falls, our 4th of the 7 natural wonders of the world Getting scuba certified and diving in Seychelles Spending another year witnessing the Running of the Bulls in Pamplona, Spain

Watching traditional Eagle Hunters share their craft in the mountains of Kyrgyzstan Riding the coveted ‘Golden Horses’ from Turkmenistan Getting an up close look at flames that give the name to The Door To Hell in Durvasa, Turkmenistan
Noma in Copenhagen exceed all expectations, and remains our favorite culinary experience for a 3 Michelin star restaurant… ever 🤯 It wasn’t a very food heavy tour, but Armenian food blew us away
We know far less about the world than we know for certain about it

p: The Camino Primitivo, the oldest of the routes — Lugo to Santiago de Compostela over six walking days, July 10th to 17th 2024. 107 kilometers on foot. We landed in Santiago, drove an hour to Lugo, and walked it backwards across Galicia through cow fields and wild blackberry hedges.

Day 0 — arrival in Lugo. Hotel Mercure Lugo. Lunch at Restaurante Campos: white wine, gazpacho, jamón ibérico, eggplant. Walked the city walls, picked up postcards, watched a video in the basement of Casa dos Mosaicos. Dinner at the hotel: zamburiñas, croquettes, green peppers, steak. White wine. Early bed.

Day 1 — the first 20.6 kilometers, Lugo to Guntín. Stretch at the Lugo sign in the city center, cross the Roman bridge, first pilgrim stamps at a church dedicated to St. Lucia, blackberries off the hedges. Lunch at Mesón de Crecente: mixed salad with bonito, cocido, carne asada, cured meats, Estrella Galicia. Dinner at Restaurant España (desde 1907). The beef rice is a sight to see.

Day 2 — 14.8km to Palas de Rei. Overcast morning. Ancient Roman bridge over Río de Ferreira (said to be where they crossed with St. James’s bones). Wild plums off the tree. Lunch at Casa Gorinos. Spa circuit and Celtic hot-stone massages at Balneario Río Pambre. A bat made five laps of the dining room while we ate dessert.

Day 3 — 15.1km to Melide. Castelo de Pambre at dawn, visited by a German Shepherd. Lunch at Garnacha Pulpería, the pulpo temple — octopus-cutting demonstration out front, beer-hall energy inside, complimentary liquor de café on the way out. Not quite sold on octopus but the room was electric.

Day 4 — 16.1km to Arzúa. Somber morning news from the U.S. Cappella de San Roque for morning stretches. Lit candles at Iglesia de Santa María de Melide to Cantus Firmus chants. Met an older gentleman we named Pepe, who told us about Día del Carmen and kissed us on the cheeks. Watched Spain beat England in the Euro Cup at a bar in Arzúa that night, joined the street party, danced in the rain with LED headbands until 4am. Menuda liada.

Day 5 — 17.6km, hungover. Warmup stretches, lunch at Marela Cantina, rain gear out. Pazo Santa María at the end — Hailey’s favorite stay. Another massage before dinner. The bass was pumping in Arzúa that night. We did not oblige.

Day 6 — 23.4km, the last day. Lunch at Casa de Amancio. First view of the Santiago cathedral from Parque Monte de Gozo. Made the 7:30pm mass, received pilgrim certificates, celebrated with locals dancing to traditional Galician music in Plaza del Obradoiro.
Day 8 — Santiago de Compostela city tour, lunch at Atafona, spa at Hotel A Quinta da Auga. Goodbyes to Gonzalo, our guide. Buen Camino.
In order of the country visited:
