Three nights on the Eastern & Oriental Express, March 18th to 21st 2024 — Singapore up through the Malaysian jungle to the northern end of peninsular Malaysia. The train greets passengers at the Singapore North Coast station with a team dressed in traditional Malay attire, caviar and champagne in the cabin, piano and saxophone in the bar car.

Out of Singapore, the windows reveal palm plantations, rural villages, construction sites, the occasional wild animal. We unpacked, dressed up, and headed to the bar car for cocktails as we passed Kulai. Evening: piano music, saxophone, drinks, the train settling into its own rhythm.

Morning one — disembark at Merapuh, where a group of fifteen locals (all under twenty) perform a song and dance. E-bikes out into the surrounding area, a rubber tree demonstration (how latex gets extracted), into the Guasisik Naga cave in Raub with helmets and headlamps, on to a cave full of bats. Local cheese tasting (sambal gouda, beemster, montasio, double Gloucester), a truck-bed ride back to the train. Lunch, nap, a little work, bar car, dinner.

Morning two — Butterworth. The train pulls in and a parade of colorful tuk-tuks waits on the platform, one per passenger. Temple in Georgetown first; Sri Maha Mariamman (Indian temple) next, with an Indian wedding under way — a beautiful sight. Temple of the Goddess of Mercy, a Buddhist temple, after that. We caved for an Oreo ice cream bar in the heat, which hit like a sugar drug. Final stop: Penang Island, a wooden-pier marketplace full of tarps and souvenirs. We could see ourselves spending more time in the hipster Victoria/35@Jetty section.
Back on the train for lunch as we crossed Bukit Merah Lake. More drinks, bar car, dinner, karaoke, sleep. Off the train the next day with new friends, incredible memories, and not-so-fun hangovers.

Five weeks across Eastern Europe and the Balkans, May 11th to June 6th 2023. We started by driving from Warsaw into Ukraine, came back, flew to Sofia, rented a car, and traced a line through North Macedonia, Albania, Montenegro, Croatia, Bosnia and Herzegovina, Serbia, Slovenia, and Slovakia. We closed the trip on the Belmond Orient Express from Budapest to Bucharest, then flew to Frankfurt to catch the plane to Nairobi and our month in Africa.

Things we’d do again: Dubrovnik for as long as possible. Sarajevo for the layered history. Lake Bled for the morning run around it. Tirana during a Colour Day Festival. Things we’d add next time: more of Montenegro, more of Croatia, and the beaches of Albania.

All the experts seek out the best bars in the world. We follow their lists and find other lists with local hidden gems and up-and-comers. We then pursue seeing what all the *buzz* is about and happen to have opinions of our own. Here’s the list we follow, and here’s how how they stack up, according to us:
Max’s List: Double Chicken Please (New York)

Six countries over five weeks, June 7th to July 8th 2023 — from Nairobi to the Seychelles, with Tanzania, Rwanda, South Africa, Zambia, and Botswana in between. A single itinerary, mostly small aircraft, and every kind of landscape the continent offers in one pass.

Fourteen Pacific-nation countries in just under eight weeks. The route: Honolulu first (the US stepping stone), then the western Pacific cluster — Palau, Micronesia, Marshall Islands, Kiribati, Nauru — before a long arc through Fiji, Solomons, Vanuatu, Samoa, Tuvalu, Tonga, Australia, New Zealand, and finally Papua New Guinea on the way to Singapore.

Most of these countries see fewer than a few thousand tourists a year. Schedules are brittle. Plan buffers. The hotels below are the ones we actually stayed in — not the ones we’d necessarily recommend.

Forty days from Cape Town to Lisbon — South Africa, Namibia, Angola, Congo, Togo, Ghana, Ivory Coast, Sierra Leone, Guinea-Bissau, and Cape Verde, along the west-African coast by ship. A coastline most travelers never touch, and our most unusual month of travel so far.

Day 1 — Cape Town. We got in a night early and stayed at the Belmond Mount Nelson. The breakfast buffet is what dreams are made of. Hailey ran around the V&A Waterfront picking up months’ worth of supplies — vitamins, On Cloud shoes, hair products, pajamas. Boarded the ship. Unpacking into a real closet for the first time in three years was its own event.

Langebaan, South Africa. Biked ~13 miles around the West Coast Peninsula — Geelbek to Stofbergsfontein, Farm 375, Lot OPGR. We started as a group of sixty; fewer than ten made it to the finish. Quiet luxury-escape town. Sore the next day.
Luderitz, Namibia. Walked around the ghost town of Kolmanskop looking for diamonds. Parts of Fallout were filmed here.

Walvis Bay, Namibia. Prop plane out of Swakopmund over the sand dunes and along the coast — seals, shipwrecks, flamingos. Max was wedged in the back with his phone dropped between the seats before takeoff, so no photos. Desert tent dinner that night with a dance performance. Next day: Dune 7 for the Walvis Bay overlook (Dune was filmed around here) and two jackals. Rode with Melanie and Kerry from Texas.

Namibe, Angola. City tour to the naval building, then out into the desert to see the endemic Welwitchsia plant (male and female), then to Tombua for rock arches and formations. Local women greeted us on the beach, some topless in traditional dress, singing and dancing.

Luanda, Angola. Old military fort, then the Slavery Museum at Benfica. Bought a wooden mask at the local market; Max made friends with a few locals walking around drinking beers asking for dollars. Security flooded the area as we left — the president was passing through. Viana for the tricolor karst at Miradouro da Lua, lunch at Kwanza Lounge on the lake. Portuguese alongside the local language.

Pointe-Noire, Congo. Realizing you’re in Congo is an event in itself. Two-hour drive to the Kouilou River, another two hours by boat into the bush — refueling with a spare tank midway — and then thirty minutes with chimpanzees on an island as local caretakers fed them fruit. One backflip, one clap for more food, a few spats. Streets felt rougher here; our police escort may or may not have been the best way to move around.

Lomé, Togo. Armed with turkey sandwiches and peanut butter English muffins, we toured four villages practicing Vodun. Dancing with the local kids was the highlight — one five-year-old guided us through his village wearing only white underpants. Then the fetish market — owl carcasses, warthog heads, anything a Vodun ritual might need. Mostly children running the stalls in the morning. Hailey played foosball in the street with Gracia and Augustine.

Takoradi and Accra, Ghana. Arrived over Easter weekend; locals gathered under tents in white marching to worship. Canopy walk at Kakum National Park. The highlight was Elmina Castle — surreal to stand in the place slaves were held and shipped to the Americas. Max made a friend with a Rastafarian local who carved a seashell with his name on it; we bought it for five dollars. In Accra: a morning hike, a visit to a glass-bead bracelet maker (Hailey bought one in every color), and lunch at the Royal Senchi — the nicest thing we’d seen in any African country on this trip.

Abidjan, Ivory Coast. National park hike. The roads were bad after rain so the buses couldn’t get close to the trailheads; we walked in. Short stop. Hailey sang Patsy Cline’s “Crazy” at karaoke that night.

Tokeh and Freetown, Sierra Leone. Dropped off the zodiac into what turned out to be a very busy local beach. Local life on full display — two men playing a chess/checkers variant, others weaving fishing nets, kids playing soccer on the sand, more men building boats. It was Sierra Leone’s Independence Day. A three-year-old shrieked in terror seeing an older white man for the first time. The blue Baptist church was packed, music pouring through the windows. That afternoon, a soccer match organized for us by The Flying Stars — amputee survivors of the civil war. Freetown the next day: a chimpanzee sanctuary, and fresh banana bread that almost stole the show.

Bijagós, Guinea-Bissau. The best time we had with locals on the trip. Kids followed us through the village the entire walk. Kids were touching Hailey’s legs as if they’d never felt fat before. On the way back, fifteen kids ran up to our zodiac with pure excitement. The next stop was another village with a beach resort — lunch, a swim, kids doing backflips on the beach. Portuguese and some French.

Praia and Porto Novo, Cape Verde. Grogue distillery visit, bought a few bottles. Hailey learned Mahjong with Carie, Brie, and Melanie on sea days. Dolphin and flying-fish sightings off the boat always caused a stir. Karaoke, 4pm trivia, evening music trivia. Chess with Max after trivia was the routine.
The short answer is everywhere. See all the countries we’ve visited, what we’ve done, where we ate, what we saw, what we recommend, and what we don’t.

Watch (or join us) as we knock as many items as we can off our experience list: Running with the bulls, hiking Everest base camp, snorkeling the Great Barrier Reef, visiting the DMZ between North and South Korea, rafting class 5 rapids in Patagonia, cruising through Antarctica, exploring the pyramids in Egypt, learning the history of mankind in the Middle East, gazing at the Northern Lights, going on safari in Africa, cruising down the Amazon River, hiking Macchu Picchu and more...
